1.exposition: The long vision of the new Jerusalem now reaches its living heart. The angel shows John a river of the water of life, bright as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb, and on either bank the tree of life, bearing twelve fruits and leaves for the healing of the nations. Everything that Eden lost is here restored and surpassed: the garden's river and tree return, now issuing from the throne itself. Then the great reversals fall like hammer-blows — no longer will there be anything accursed; God's servants will serve him; they will see his face and bear his name; and there is no night, no lamp, no sun, for the Lord God will be their light, and they will reign forever and ever. This is the church's hope at its summit — the unmediated presence of God, the beatific vision, and a healed, reigning humanity in a world where the curse is undone.
1.original_language: The river is a potamos hydatos zōēs (“a river of water of life”), lampron hōs krystallon (“bright as crystal”) — not a still pool but a moving stream of grace. The tree is the xylon zōēs (“tree of life,” Genesis 2:9); xylon means “wood” as well as “tree,” the very word the New Testament uses of the cross — the wood of death becoming, at the last, the tree of life. Its leaves are for the therapeia (“healing, therapy”) of the nations. In v.3 pan katathema ouk estai eti may be rendered “no more curse” or “nothing accursed,” echoing the Zechariah 14:11 promise. And opsontai to prosōpon autou (v.4, “they shall see his face”) grants what was once impossible.
1.historical_context: Every image is drawn from Israel's Scriptures, and together they make this vision the great inclusio of the whole Bible. Scripture opens in a garden with a river parting into streams and the tree of life at its center (Genesis 2:9; Genesis 2:10), and closes in a garden-city with river and tree restored — the flaming sword of Genesis 3:24 withdrawn, access reopened. The river fulfills Ezekiel's temple-stream that deepens and brings life wherever it flows, lined with trees whose leaves heal (Ezekiel 47:1; Ezekiel 47:12), and the river of God in Psalm 46:4. The early Latin commentator latin-fathers Bede read the river and tree sacramentally, of baptism and the Spirit's life in the Church, while the broader tradition saw the consummated communion of God with his people.
1.christ: The river flows from a single throne — the throne of God and of the Lamb — one seat shared by Father and Son, the strongest possible affirmation of the Lamb's deity. The water of life is the Spirit Christ promised would flow from within those who believe (John 7:38–39; John 4:14), and the tree of life is enjoyed by those washed in the Lamb's blood. To see his face is to behold the glory of God in the face of Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6; 1 John 3:2). The Lamb who was slain is himself the lamp of the city, so that the goal of all redemption is unending sight of the crucified and risen Lord.

verses.note: The vision reaches its center: a river of the water of life, bright as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read the potamos hydatos zōēs as the abundant, life-giving grace of the new creation, fulfilling Ezekiel's temple-river (Ezekiel 47:1) and the living waters of Zechariah 14:8. That it proceeds from one throne “of God and of the Lamb” the latin-fathers Bede and eastern Cyril take as the grace of the Spirit streaming from Father and Son together — the deity of the Lamb quietly asserted by the single, shared throne.

On either side of the river stands the tree of life, bearing twelve kinds of fruit, its leaves for the healing of the nations. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear Eden's tree (Genesis 2:9), lost at the fall, now restored — the Greek xylon (“tree, wood”) later the word for the cross. The perpetual fruit, note Barnes and Ellicott, figures the unfailing supply of life; and the leaves' therapeia, the eastern Cyril reads as the wholeness ministered to the redeemed nations — the wound of the peoples made well (Ezekiel 47:12).

No longer will there be anything accursed. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the reversal of Genesis 3:17 — the curse gone, as promised in Zechariah 14:11. In its place “the throne of God and of the Lamb will be in it, and his servants will worship him”; the blessedness of the redeemed, note Barnes and Ellicott, is no idleness but glad, priestly service in the immediate presence of God.

They will see his face — opsontai to prosōpon autou — “and his name will be on their foreheads.” The eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical Barnes hold this the beatific vision: the sight of God forbidden to mortal Moses (Exodus 33:20) is granted to the glorified (1 Corinthians 13:12; 1 John 3:2). The name on the forehead, the grammatical voices add, is the seal of God's ownership — the redeemed marked wholly his, the exact counter-sign to the beast's brand.

There will be no night there; no lamp, no sun is needed, “for the Lord God will be their light, and they will reign forever and ever.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott resume the light of Isaiah 60:19 — God himself the everlasting day. And the redeemed reign, Robertson notes, eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn; the creation mandate to rule (Genesis 1:26–28) is perfected at last in the saints who share the kingdom (Daniel 7:18).

witnesses.point: Reads the river and the tree sacramentally — the grace of the Spirit and the life of baptism flowing from God and the Lamb to the Church.
The leaves are for the healing of the nations, and “they shall see his face” is the beatific vision — the sight of God granted to the glorified.
The river figures the abundant, life-giving grace of the new creation; “no more curse” reverses the fall, and God himself is the eternal light.
The tree's monthly fruit figures the unfailing supply of life; the created lights are superseded because the Lord God gives them light.

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.point: Reads the river-and-tree vision as Eden restored and expanded into an end-time temple-city — the garden sanctuary now filling the whole of the new creation, saturated with Ezekiel's temple imagery.

1.application: The whole Bible arcs from a garden humanity was driven out of to a garden-city thrown open, and its center is a face. For any believer under trial the counsel is the book's own: lift your eyes — the end of the story is not the lake of fire but the unveiled face of God, and present suffering is the road that leads to that throne.

6.exposition: The book turns to its epilogue, and the epilogue answers the prologue. The angel certifies that these words are trustworthy and true, and Jesus himself breaks in with the first of three great refrains: Behold, I am coming soon. John, undone by the glory, again falls to worship the angel and is again rebuked — Worship God!. Unlike Daniel's scroll, this one is not sealed, for the time is near. Christ comes bringing recompense (v.12), names himself the Alpha and the Omega, the first and the last, the beginning and the end, pronounces the seventh beatitude on those who wash their robes, and seals the whole book with his own titles: the root and the offspring of David, the bright morning star. The thread is authentication and imminence — the visions are true, sealed by the returning Christ, and they summon obedient watchfulness now.
6.original_language: Three times the risen Lord says erchomai tachy — “I am coming soon / swiftly” (vv.7, 12, 20) — the refrain that frames the close. In v.13 the divine self-title is threefold: to Alpha kai to Ō (the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet), ho prōtos kai ho eschatos (“the first and the last”), hē archē kai to telos (“the beginning and the end”). The famous crux of v.14 turns on two similar Greek phrases — hoi plynontes tas stolas (“those who wash their robes”) versus hoi poiountes tas entolas (“those who do his commandments”). And Christ's titles in v.16 are hē rhiza kai to genos David (“root and offspring of David”) and ho astēr ho lampros ho prōinos (“the bright morning star”).
6.historical_context: The threefold “I am coming soon” would steel churches under Domitian's pressure: their vindication is near. The command not to seal the book (v.10) deliberately reverses Daniel 12:4, where Daniel was told to seal his words “until the time of the end” — for John that end has dawned in Christ, so the prophecy stays open and proclaimed. John's mistaken worship of the angel (vv.8–9), repeating his lapse at Revelation 19:10, reads as a pointed first-century polemic against the angel-veneration the church had been warned of (Colossians 2:18). The “morning star” and “root of David” titles reach back to Numbers 24:17 and Isaiah 11:1, 10.
6.christ: This passage is one of the New Testament's densest concentrations of Christ's self-disclosed deity. He claims the divine title the Alpha and the Omega, the first and the last, spoken by the LORD in Isaiah 44:6 and Isaiah 48:12, and already given to God in Revelation 1:8 and 21:6. He is at once David's root and descendant — both David's source and David's son, the riddle of Matthew 22:42–45 — and “the bright morning star,” herald of the eternal day. He comes to repay each one for what he has done, exercising the divine prerogative of judgment; and the whole book is finally his testimony to the churches, the self-revelation of the risen Lord.

verses.note: The epilogue opens as the prologue did. These words are trustworthy and true: grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear the seal of authenticity (as 21:5; 19:9). That “the Lord God of the spirits of the prophets sent his angel,” puritan Matthew Henry notes, sets this revelation in the line of the prophetic word — the same God who inspired the prophets speaks here. The “things that must soon take place” reach back to the book's opening (1:1): the fulfillment is near.

Jesus breaks in: Behold, I am coming soon — erchomai tachy, the first of three in this chapter (vv.12, 20). grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read tachy of the swiftness and certainty of his coming, the church's perpetual expectation. The attached beatitude — “blessed is the one who keeps the words of the prophecy of this book” — is the sixth of the book's seven (echoing 1:3): the visions are given to be obeyed, not merely read.

I, John, am the one who heard and saw these things. Barnes and Robertson mark the eyewitness attestation of the apostle, sealing the reality of the visions. Then, overwhelmed by their glory, John falls to worship at the feet of the interpreting angel — the second such lapse in the book (as 19:10).

You must not do that! the angel says: “I am a fellow servant with you and your brethren the prophets, and with those who keep the words of this book. Worship God.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the angel ranks himself a creature-servant beside John and the saints; the repeated rebuke (as 19:10) guards the first commandment — worship belongs to God alone, never to any messenger however glorious.

Do not seal up the words of the prophecy of this book, for the time is near. The grammatical voices, Barnes and Ellicott, set this against Daniel, who was told to seal his words “until the time of the end” (Daniel 12:4): what for Daniel was distant has, for John, dawned in Christ — so the scroll stays open, accessible, and proclaimed to the church now.

Let the evildoer still do evil... and the righteous still do right. reformed Gill and grammatical-historical Barnes refer the solemn word to the scenes beyond judgment, when probation is past and character is fixed — not a license to sin but a declaration of the irreversible divide: the holy sealed forever in holiness, the unjust in their sin.

Behold, I am coming soon, bringing my recompense — the second “I am coming soon,” “to repay each one for what he has done.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note that Christ exercises the divine prerogative of recompense (Isaiah 40:10; Matthew 16:27; Romans 2:6): the works are the measure of the judgment, though — as the next verse's beatitude will show — not its ground.

I am the Alpha and the Omega, the first and the last, the beginning and the end. grammatical-historical Robertson and Barnes press the threefold title of eternity — to Alpha kai to Ō, ho prōtos kai ho eschatos, hē archē kai to telos: what God claims in 1:8 and 21:6, and what the LORD speaks in Isaiah 44:6, Jesus here says of himself — an unmistakable claim to be the eternal God.

Blessed are those who wash their robes — the seventh and last beatitude, and a famous textual crux. The reformed Gill and wesleyan-arminian Clarke and Wesley follow the Received Text's hoi poiountes tas entolas (“those who do his commandments”); the grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson, with the older manuscripts, read hoi plynontes tas stolas (“those who wash their robes” in the Lamb's blood, 7:14). Either way, note Barnes and Ellicott, the promise is one: the faithful — obedient and cleansed — have right to the tree of life (v.2) and entrance through the gates into the city.

Outside are the dogs and sorcerers. grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Gill take “dogs” as the impure and shameless (Philippians 3:2); with the sorcerers, the sexually immoral, murderers, idolaters, and “everyone who loves and practices falsehood” (as 21:8), they are the unredeemed shut out — the holy city admits nothing false or unclean.

I, Jesus, have sent my angel to testify these things to you for the churches. The Lord seals the whole book with his own name. Barnes and Ellicott note the two titles: he is “the root and the offspring of David” — hē rhiza kai to genos David, at once David's source and David's son, the riddle of Matthew 22:42–45 (5:5; Isaiah 11:1, 10) — and “the bright morning star” (Numbers 24:17; 2 Peter 1:19), herald of the eternal dawn his coming brings.

witnesses.point: In v.13 the threefold title of eternity — Alpha and Omega, first and last, beginning and end — is claimed by Jesus; and the older manuscripts read “wash their robes” in v.14.
“I am coming soon” marks the certainty of Christ's coming; he brings his recompense, and his titles (root of David, morning star) fulfill Numbers 24 and Isaiah 11.
The angel's certification sets the revelation in the line of the holy prophets, and the beatitude blesses the one who keeps — not merely reads — the words of the prophecy.
The fixity of moral states (v.11) refers to the scenes beyond judgment, when probation is over; the dogs and sorcerers are the unredeemed excluded from the city.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: Hears in the divine titles — Alpha and Omega, first and last, beginning and end — Christ deliberately included within the unique identity of the one God of Israel, sharing the eternity and sovereignty of the LORD.

6.application: The nearness of Christ's coming is meant to produce vigilance and holiness, not date-setting. “Blessed is the one who keeps the words of the prophecy” (v.7): Revelation is given not to satisfy curiosity but to be obeyed. And the only entrance requirement is to wash your robes — cleansing in the Lamb's blood, never personal merit; the works Christ repays are the evidence, not the ground, of that grace.

17.exposition: The Bible ends in invitation and longing. The Spirit and the Bride say, “Come”; and the one who hears is to say “Come”; and the thirsty are bidden to come and take the water of life without price. A solemn warning follows against adding to or taking from the words of the prophecy (vv.18–19). Then the final exchange — the risen Lord's last word in all Scripture and the church's answer: “Surely I am coming soon.” Amen. Come, Lord Jesus! — and the benediction, the grace of the Lord Jesus be with all. The whole vision of the end issues in a double cry: the gospel invitation outward to the thirsty, and the church's answering prayer upward for the Bridegroom's return. After the bowls and the lake of fire, the Bible's last note to the world is not threat but welcome, and its last word of all is grace.
17.original_language: The invitation is carried by a single imperative, erchou (“Come!”): the Spirit and the Bride say it, and the one who hears is to echo it; then erchesthō (“let him come”) for the thirsty and labetō (“let him take”) the water of life dōrean (“freely, as a gift, without price”) — the free offer of Isaiah 55:1. The warning turns on two verbs: if anyone epithē (“adds”) or aphelē (“takes away”) from the words. Christ's closing promise is nai, erchomai tachy (“Yes, I am coming soon”), and the church answers Amēn, erchou kyrie Iēsou — the Greek of the ancient Aramaic prayer Marana tha (“Our Lord, come,” 1 Corinthians 16:22). The book ends, fittingly, on hē charis — grace.
17.historical_context: “Come, Lord Jesus” renders the Aramaic Marana tha, one of the earliest Christian prayers, preserved untranslated in 1 Corinthians 16:22 — evidence that longing for Christ's return was woven into the worship of the first churches, including the eucharistic gathering. The free offer of “the water of life without price” echoes Isaiah 55:1, “come to the waters... without money.” The warning against tampering with the words (vv.18–19) follows the covenant formula of Deuteronomy 4:2 and Deuteronomy 12:32; in its original setting it guards this particular prophecy against alteration by copyists, not (as later sometimes assumed) the closing of the whole canon — though it has rightly been heard as honoring the integrity of God's word.
17.christ: Christ has the first and last word: he is the one who testifies to these things, and his final promise is “Surely I am coming soon.” The Bride longs for her Bridegroom; the marriage supper of the Lamb (Revelation 19:7–9) is the consummation toward which the prayer reaches. The water of life without price is given by the Lamb who purchased it at infinite cost — free to us because paid by him (Revelation 5:9). So the Bible ends as it must, with the grace of the Lord Jesus: the whole story, from Eden's lost tree to the city's restored one, is the story of his redeeming grace.

verses.note: The Spirit and the Bride say, “Come” — erchou. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear the Spirit and the church joined in one voice, calling — whether the thirsty to Christ or Christ to come, or both. The call then widens: “let the one who hears say, ‘Come,’” and Ellicott notes every hearer is to join the invitation; “let the one who is thirsty come; let the one who desires take the water of life without price” — hydōr zōēs dōrean, the free, universal offer of Isaiah 55:1 and John 7:37, salvation as sheer gift.

I warn everyone who hears the words of the prophecy of this book: if anyone adds to them, God will add to him the plagues written in it. Barnes and Ellicott set the warning in the covenant formula of Deuteronomy 4:2 and Deuteronomy 12:32: the integrity of the prophecy is guarded, and nothing is to be added to it.

And if anyone takes away from the words... God will take away his share in the tree of life and in the holy city. grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Gill note the matching penalty of forfeited blessing; the twofold guard — against adding and against subtracting — preserves the word entire, a solemnity aimed first at copyists yet rightly heard as honoring the whole of God's word.

He who testifies to these things says, “Surely I am coming soon” — nai, erchomai tachy, the third and last “I am coming soon,” the risen Lord's closing word in all Scripture. Barnes and puritan Matthew Henry hear the church answer with “Amen. Come, Lord Jesus!” — erchou kyrie Iēsou, the Greek of the ancient Aramaic Maranatha (1 Corinthians 16:22): the bride's believing, longing prayer answering the Bridegroom's promise.

The grace of the Lord Jesus be with all. Amen. Barnes and Robertson note that the book — and with it the whole canon of Scripture — closes as an epistle, with the apostolic benediction of grace (as Romans 16:20). The prophecy of judgment and glory ends, the eastern Cyril observes, sealed with the church's “Amen” — so that the Bible's very last word is grace.

witnesses.point: The Spirit and the Bride call all to come and take the water of life freely — the gracious, universal gospel invitation — and the book closes with the apostolic benediction of grace.
Every hearer is to join the invitation “Come”; the twofold warning against adding and taking away guards the integrity of the prophecy.
To Christ's “Surely I come quickly” the church answers “Amen. Even so, come, Lord Jesus” — the believing prayer of longing, and grace is the fitting last word.
The closing “Amen” seals the benediction and the whole book, so that Scripture ends in the grace of the Lord Jesus and the church's assent.

external.name: Craig R. Koester
external.point: Sets “Come, Lord Jesus” within the worship of the earliest churches — the Maranatha prayer of the assembly, where the invitation to the thirsty and the longing for Christ's return met at the Lord's table.

17.application: The last invitation in Scripture is not a threat but an open door: come, take the water of life, without price (v.17). The church's calling is twofold — to echo that invitation to every thirsty soul, and to pray, with the whole suffering people of God, “Come, Lord Jesus” (v.20). Notice that the prophecy is not finished until the hearer joins the cry; the book waits for your “Amen.”

_meta.title: Behold, I Am Coming Soon
reflection: Scripture opens in a garden with a river and the tree of life (Genesis 2:9–10) and closes in a garden-city with both restored and the curse undone (Revelation 22:2–3). How does seeing the whole Bible arc from Eden to the new Jerusalem — from a tree lost to a tree given — change the way you read the story you are living inside?

“They will see his face” (Revelation 22:4) is the church's summit of hope — the sight of God once forbidden to Moses (Exodus 33:20) now the saints' eternal portion. What in your life would change today if you truly lived toward the unveiled face of God as your final and certain end?

The Spirit and the Bride say “Come” to the thirsty, and the church answers “Come, Lord Jesus” to the Bridegroom (Revelation 22:17; 22:20). Which cry comes harder for you — the invitation outward to someone thirsty, or the longing upward for Christ's return — and what would it take to make both your own?
